data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Harness strictly beats self-consistency only when the critic is question-adaptive: it must concentrate recall on questions where the candidate majority is fragile (margin ≤ 1) and concentrate precision on questions where the majority is stable, yielding strict benefit over both fixed critics and self-consistency. A fixed-precision critic cannot strictly beat self-consistency in any regime.", "mechanism": "A fixed critic applies its recall and falseflag rate uniformly across all questions. On stable-majority questions, any nonzero falseflag rate breaks correct majorities with no compensating benefit (no wrong answers to catch). On fragile-majority questions, recall must be high enough to flip them. A fixed critic faces a binding tradeoff: raising recall to help fragile questions raises falseflag harm on stable ones. An adaptive critic decouples these: it can set near-zero falseflag on stable-majority questions (precision→1) while setting high recall on fragile-majority questions. The strict benefit is the adaptive-critic gain minus the fixed-critic gain, which is positive whenever the question distribution mixes stable and fragile majorities (which it always does at finite k). The causal mechanism is information-theoretic: the critic observes an additional signal (its own assessment) that is correlated with majority fragility, and conditioning on it strictly improves the filtering policy.", "scope": {"covers": ["any k ≥ 5", "any e in (0,1) where the question distribution includes both stable and fragile majorities", "critic with access to a fragility-correlated signal (e.g., its own confidence, candidate agreement pattern, or a separate probe)"], "excludes": ["fixed-precision/recall critics (by hypothesis they cannot strictly beat SC)", "uniform-error questions where all majorities have identical fragility"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "A fixed-precision critic with ANY recall/precision combination strictly beats self-consistency at k=7, e=0.33 — if so, the question-adaptivity claim is unnecessary and the candidate is refuted", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "An adaptive critic using a fragility signal achieves the SAME harness accuracy as a fixed critic with identical marginal recall and falseflag rates — if so, adaptivity provides no additional benefit and the mechanism is refuted", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "The adaptive critic's fragility signal is shown to be uncorrelated with actual majority fragility (p > 0.05 on a held-out calibration set), yet harness still strictly beats SC — if so, the causal mechanism is wrong", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This candidate negates the fixed-critic abstraction directly, arguing that strict benefit requires question-adaptive critic behavior. The decisive experiment uses k=7, e≈0.33 (mid-zone from candidate 2d711b2e), with 3 critic arms: (1) fixed critic at recall=0.6/precision=0.5, (2) adaptive critic using candidate-agreement as fragility signal, (3) adaptive critic using critic-confidence as fragility signal. Each arm runs 120 items × 5 seeds = 600 samples, ~180k tokens, within budget. The key test is whether adaptive arms strictly beat both the fixed critic and SC while fixed critic does not beat SC."}
